title: Everett
name: Everett
metaDescription: 1031 exchange coordination for Everett's Boeing-anchored aerospace industrial, Paine Field suppliers, and waterfront redevelopment near Seattle.

sections.html: Everett's commercial base is built more narrowly around aerospace than any other city in the metro, and a buyer assembling an Everett identification package is typically choosing between Boeing-adjacent industrial space, Paine Field supplier facilities, or waterfront redevelopment further from the airport. A qualified intermediary coordinating the closing should confirm which of the three a target property belongs to before the 45-day identification window closes, since each carries a different tenant pool and lease structure.

sections.heading: Boeing's Everett Plant and the Supplier Base It Supports
sections.html:
Boeing's Everett assembly plant is one of the largest manufacturing buildings in the world, and the industrial space surrounding it leases heavily to suppliers and subcontractors whose fortunes track Boeing's production schedule for its widebody aircraft programs. A buyer identifying supplier-adjacent industrial should ask directly whether a prospective tenant's business depends on a single Boeing program, since a production-rate change on that program can affect the tenant's ability to renew.

Clear height, crane capacity, and power service matter more in Everett's aerospace-supplier buildings than in a conventional distribution warehouse, and a buyer should confirm those specifications separately rather than assuming a generic industrial building will suit an aerospace subcontractor without retrofitting.

A buyer should also confirm whether a supplier tenant's lease includes any equipment or fixture arrangements tied to a specific Boeing contract, since those arrangements can complicate a straightforward re-lease if the current tenant's contract ends before its lease term does.

sections.heading: Paine Field and the Wider Supplier Corridor
sections.html:
Paine Field supports a wider ring of aerospace suppliers, maintenance operations, and flight-test tenants beyond Boeing's own plant, and this corridor has also added scheduled passenger service in recent years, which has pulled in a smaller set of airport-adjacent retail and hospitality uses. A buyer should treat the aerospace-supplier stock and the newer passenger-terminal-adjacent commercial space as separate underwriting exercises, since the tenant pools barely overlap.

Everett's supplier corridor trades at a discount to Kent Valley industrial on a per-square-foot basis, largely because the tenant base is narrower and more cyclical, and a buyer should reflect that concentration risk in the cap rate rather than pricing off a diversified-industrial comparable.

sections.heading: Waterfront Redevelopment Beyond the Aerospace Corridor
sections.html:
Away from the Boeing plant, Everett's waterfront and downtown core have added newer mixed-use and multifamily development aimed at a renter base priced out of Seattle and Bellevue, and this inventory behaves like a conventional suburban multifamily market rather than an aerospace-dependent one. A buyer replacing into this pocket should confirm current rent-cap treatment and concession levels the same way they would anywhere else in the metro, since the aerospace cycle has little bearing on this specific product type.

Naval Station Everett also sits along this same waterfront corridor, and while the base itself is not an available replacement asset, its presence supports a modest additional layer of nearby retail and service demand that a buyer can factor into a downtown Everett retail underwriting narrative. This downtown pocket typically trades at a lower basis than comparable Seattle or Bellevue multifamily, which can make it a workable landing spot for a buyer managing boot exposure on a downsizing exchange out of a higher-value relinquished property.

sections.heading: Building the Everett Identification List
sections.html:
A workable Everett submittal typically draws from:

  • Boeing-adjacent industrial and manufacturing-support space
  • Paine Field aerospace-supplier buildings
  • waterfront or downtown multifamily unrelated to the aerospace corridor
  • neighborhood retail serving the surrounding residential population

sections.heading: Sequencing Around Concentration Risk
sections.html:
Because so much of Everett's industrial base ties back to a single employer's production schedule, a buyer should be direct with a qualified intermediary and lender about how concentrated a target property's tenant roster is before finalizing an identification, since that concentration can affect both loan terms and insurance pricing.

Buyers frequently pair an Everett aerospace-supplier identification with a Lynnwood or Bothell alternative on the same three-property list, keeping a less concentrated submarket available as a fallback inside the 180-day exchange period. If a buyer identifies more than three Everett-area candidates, the 200% and 95% identification rules become the relevant test, and a qualified intermediary should confirm the combined value or eventual acquisition percentage before the 45-day window closes.

faqs.q: How dependent is Everett's industrial market on a single employer?
faqs.a: Heavily, since Boeing's Everett plant and its widebody production schedule support most of the aerospace-supplier leasing in the surrounding buildings, which is a concentration risk a buyer should reflect in underwriting.

faqs.q: What should a buyer confirm before identifying an aerospace-supplier building?
faqs.a: Clear height, crane capacity, and power service, along with whether the current tenant's business depends on a single Boeing production program that could change rate, and whether any tenant-installed equipment or fixture arrangements are tied specifically to that program's contract term.

faqs.q: Has passenger service at Paine Field changed the surrounding commercial market?
faqs.a: It has added a smaller ring of airport-adjacent retail and hospitality demand, but this pool barely overlaps with the aerospace-supplier tenant base and should be underwritten separately.

faqs.q: Is Everett multifamily tied to the aerospace cycle?
faqs.a: Not directly, waterfront and downtown multifamily here behaves like a conventional suburban market and should be underwritten on rent-cap treatment and concessions rather than aerospace production trends.

faqs.q: Why pair an Everett identification with Lynnwood or Bothell?
faqs.a: Both carry less production-cycle concentration than Everett's aerospace-supplier stock, giving a buyer a workable fallback if a single-tenant concentration issue complicates financing on the Everett candidate.
